Deacidification of concentrated cranberry juice by electrodialysis withbipolar membranes: A feasibility and comprehensive study Romain Canuel , ´El´ea Fynaut , V´eronique Perreault , Laurent Bazinet * Laboratoire de Transformation Alimentaire et Proc´ed´es ´ElectroMembranaires (LTAPEM, Laboratory of Food Processing and ElectroMembrane Processes), Institute ofNutrition and Functional Foods (INAF), Department of Food Sciences, Universit´e Laval, Quebec, QC, G1V 0A6, Canada

A R T I C L E I N F O Keywords:ElectrodialysisBipolar membranesDeacidificationConcentrated cranberry juiceConductivity

citation.title: Deacidification of concentrated cranberry juice by electrodialysis with bipolar membranes: A feasibility and comprehensive study

content:
A B S T R A C T Electrodialysis with bipolar membranes (EDBM) recently shown promising outcomes as a novel green technologyto deacidify cranberry juice without altering its polyphenolic compounds, responsible for many health benefits.The performance of EDBM depends on many factors, including the concentration of the treated solution. Whileprevious research only deacidified cranberry juice by EDBM with concentration of 8◦B, the present study aimedto assess the physicochemical attributes of more concentrated juices and the effect of using specific ones (5◦B,15◦B, 25◦B and 35◦B) during EDBM on deacidification and electrodialytic parameters evolution. Optimal juicecharacteristics for EDBM use (viscosity, conductivity) were exhibited at 25◦B. The 4 selected juice concentrationswere successfully deacidified by 39.0 ± 2.5 % but with significantly different process times (from 46 ± 2 min to471 ± 16 min). Extended treatment duration led to a slight loss in anthocyanins and proanthocyanidins, likelydue to increased polyphenols-membranes interactions, which contributed to system resistance growth. However,the reduction in citric (32 ± 3 %) and malic (61 ± 4 %) acids remained consistent, regardless of the juiceconcentration. This work confirmed the feasibility of producing a concentrated and deacidified cranberry juiceby EDBM, that can represent a great potential to the functional food market.

* Corresponding author.E-mail address: laurent.bazinet@fsaa.ulaval.ca (L. Bazinet).

content: 13], including electromembrane processes. These properties are mainlydependent on the temperature, composition, and soluble solid content ofthe juice [14,15]. In concentrated juices, higher conductivity and betterED performances would be expected [16], as previously exhibited fordairy whey demineralization [17,18] and tofu whey protein extraction[19]. Increasing feed conductivity would decrease the ED process energyconsumption (EC = UIt) as lower voltage would need to be applied witha constant intensity [20]. In addition, a high conductivity system contributes to a large-scale migration of ions, which is sought for ED processes [17]. However, similar phenomena still need to be demonstratedon fruit juice matrices and for ED deacidification purposes. Consequently, the impact of higher total soluble content and possibly viscosityof cranberry juice on the EDBM process should be studied regarding thepotential process optimization and sales opportunity for a deacidifiedand concentrated cranberry juice.

content: he EDBM process should be studied regarding thepotential process optimization and sales opportunity for a deacidifiedand concentrated cranberry juice. Transport and storage of concentratedcranberry juice would also be easier for the industry.In this context, the objectives of the present work were to 1) understand the effect of concentration (in terms of Brix degree) on cranberry juice main physicochemical properties (density, total polyphenolscontent, pH, titratable acidity, viscosity, conductivity), 2) identify thebest concentrations of juice to be treated by EDBM, 3) investigate theimpact of juice concentrations during EDBM on physicochemical andcomposition evolution of juice and organic acids recovery solution and4) study the impact on electrodialytic performances. 2. Materials and methods 2.1. Cranberry juice Pasteurized and clarified concentrated cranberry juice (50◦B) wasprovided by Fruit d’Or (Plessisville, Quebec, Canada). The juice wasstored at -30 ◦C and thawed at 4 ◦C before each experiment. The maincharacteristics of the concentrated cranberry juice were determined andare presented in Table 1.

Cranberry juice physicochemical properties Prior to ED trials, cranberry juice physicochemical properties (density, total polyphenols content, pH, titratable acidity, viscosity, conductivity) were assessed at different juice concentrations, expressed asBrix degree (◦B). Various dilutions were made from the initial concentrated cranberry juice (50◦B) with distilled water. Analyses were carriedout in triplicate at room temperature (22 ± 1 ◦C) and dilution factorswere confirmed using a pocket refractometer model PAL-1 (Atago,Tokyo, Japan). 2.3. Electrodialysis experiments 2.3.1. Electrodialysis cell and configurationED experiments were performed using an EUR-2 pilot-scale cell(Eurodia, Pertuis, France), with an effective membrane surface area of200 cm2, following the configuration described by Faucher et al. [10](Fig. 1). Seven anion-exchange membranes (AEM, Astom Corporation,Tokyo, Japan), seven bipolar membranes (BPM, Astom Corporation),and two cation-exchange membranes (CEM, Astom Corporation) werestacked in between the electrodes (Dimensionally Stable Electrode,DSE). This configuration resulted in three separate compartments:cranberry juice, organic acids recovery solution, and electrode rinsingsolution. Migration of anions and organic acids anions was possiblethrough the AEM, while production of OH-in the juice compartmentand H+ in the recovery compartment was ensured by the BPM thanks tothe water molecule dissociation.

Deacidification evolution was monitored byregular titratable acidity measurements from the juice compartment.Several physicochemical parameters (flow rate, temperature, conductivity, pH) were followed in the three compartments throughout theprocess. Collection of samples from the juice and recovery compartments was carried out at the initial and final time for anthocyanins,PACs, and organic acids content analyses. All samples collected werekept at -30 ◦C and thawed at 4 ◦C before analyses.The recovery and the electrode rinsing compartments were respectively filled with solutions of KCl 2 g/L and NaOH 0.1 M as already reported [21]. An equal volume of 3 L was circulated in each compartmentand remained constant during treatment. A constant pressure of 27.6 kPaand temperature of 21 ± 2 ◦C was maintained in each compartment.Current intensity andvoltage were recorded directlyonthe powersupply.Constant current was applied during the process until voltage reached 24V, following the manufacturer instructions. The ED system was cleanedusing HCl 0.1 M and NaOH 0.1 M solutions between each experiment,while KCl solution with conductivity of 20 mS/cm was employed as astorage solution. The same set of membranes was used to perform allexperiments and was disassembled afterward to be characterized for theircolor, thickness, and conductivity.

content: 3. Results and discussion 3.1. Effect of cranberry juice concentration on juice physicochemicalproperties 3.1.1. DensityCranberry juice density exhibited a positive linear relationship(r2 = 0.989) with its concentration (Fig. 2A). Density increased significantly (p < 0.001) from 1011.7 ± 0.7 kg/m3 to 1231.5 ± 12.7 kg/m3 within the concentration range 5◦B - 50◦B. No information about densityand dilution is reported in the literature for cranberry juice, but such alinear increase has already been observed for orange [29], apple [30],and grape juices [31]. Data obtained in the present work for cranberryjuice (slope of 4.67 kg/m3◦B-1 at 22 ◦C) is consistent with those previousstudies considering that temperature affects density (reported slopes of4.62–5.40 kg/m3◦B-1 at 20 ◦C–25 ◦C) [29–31]. This behavior wouldmean that density is directly related to the total soluble solid contents ofthe cranberry concentrate. For the same volume of juice, it may besuggested that the less diluted the juice is, the more molecules (sugars,organic acids, minerals, polyphenols, etc.) are present, which results inhigher mass and thus density (Equation (1)). Zuritz et al. [32] indicatedthat clear grape juice density was strongly affected by sugars concentrations as these compounds are the main solid constituents of the juice. 3.1.2.

content: dthat clear grape juice density was strongly affected by sugars concentrations as these compounds are the main solid constituents of the juice. 3.1.2. Total polyphenolsConsistently with density results, total polyphenol content in 5◦B to50◦B cranberry juice evolved linearly (r2 = 0.99) from 624 ± 4 mg/L to7447 ± 242 mg/L (p < 0.001) respectively (Fig. 2B). Therefore, dilutionof the concentrated cranberry juice did not affect the form of the totalpolyphenols, their concentration was just diluted. 3.1.3. pH and titratable acidityCranberry juice pH decreased significantly from 2.50 ± 0.05 to2.04 ± 0.02 (p < 0.001) according to the juice concentration (Fig. 2C).As expected from previous studies on cranberry juice (Table 1), the morediluted the juice is, the fewer free H3O+ ions are present in solution,which increases the pH. However, the pH evolution did not follow alinear trend as it could be expected but rather evolved in a logarithmicform. This is consistent with the pH calculation formula that refers to thenegative logarithmic function (base 10) of the H3O+ ions concentration.Furthermore, if H3O+ ions concentration is back calculated with the pHformula and plotted against juice concentration, a linear increase isobtained (r2 = 0.99), suggesting that H3O+ concentration was simplydiluted. A similar linear increase (r2 = 0.99) in juice titratable aciditywas also obtained from 5◦B to 50◦B. Consistent results were reported onpassion fruit juice [16].

This evolution supports the above assumptionabout dilution, even though titratable acidity measures total acid concentration in juice by titration rather than free H3O+ ions. 3.1.4. ViscosityAsignificantriseofviscosityfrom1.28±0.03mPa.sto10.93 ± 0.13 mPa.s (p < 0.001) was measured with a constant shear rate(100 s-1) as cranberry juice concentration increased (Fig. 3A). Viscositybehavior seemed to deviate from 25◦B where an inflection point can beoutlined: viscosity increased by a factor of 1.81 below this concentrationand 4.71 above, where the concentration has a more important effect onviscosity. Such a consequent growth in viscosity over a specific concentration threshold has already been observed on several fruit juices(pomegranate, pear, lemon, tangerine, grape) measured at 20 ◦C–30 ◦C[14,32,33]. Considering that temperature and juice composition canaffect viscosity, this phenomenon is consistent with data obtained forcranberry juice.Regarding the evolution of shear stress according to shear rate(Fig. 3B), the results showed a linear relationship (r2 > 0.99) betweenthose parameters whatever the juice concentration. This suggests thatcranberry juice behaves as a Newtonian fluid from 5◦B to 50◦B. Flow

An optimum was reached at 25◦B with a conductivityvalue of 3.99 ± 0.03 mS/cm, which is about 1.8 times of the lowest value.The conductivity measurement considers the number of electricalcharges or charged molecules present in the solution and their electrophoretic mobility [15].

content: nductivity measurement considers the number of electricalcharges or charged molecules present in the solution and their electrophoretic mobility [15]. Therefore, the juice chemical composition (ionsnature) and viscosity can have a significant impact on conductivity.Several charged species in cranberry juice can affect the conductivitysuch as minerals, of which potassium (K+) is the major component, anthocyanins, and PACs that are positively charged, and organics acids thatare partially ionized (negatively charged) in the juice pH [8]. From thosecompounds, specific attention can be given to organic acids as their ionicdistributions are affected by the pH evolution observed in the juice concentration range (pH of 2.50 to 2.04). Citric (pKa1 = 3.13, pKa2 = 4.76,pKa3 = 6.39), quinic (pKa = 3.46), and malic acids (pKa1 = 3.46,pKa2 = 5.05) are the main organic acids found in the cranberry juice andcan be present in different proportion of ionized and non-ionized formsaccording to their respective pKa and juice pH [8]. The percentage ofH2Cit-is approximately 4 times more present at5◦B than 50◦B(higher pHat 5◦B), increasing from 5 % to 21.5 %. The same applies for quinic andmalic acid since their respective ionized forms Quin-(C7H11O6-) andHmal-(C4H5O5-) are 3.6 times more present in the juice at 5◦B than 50◦B,increasing from 2.5 % to 9 % [8].

Their dissociation influences conductivity as additional charges (H3O+ and COO-ions) are released.Such dissociation mechanism should be considered in parallel tojuice viscosity for interpretation of the parabolic-shaped conductivityevolution. When diluting the concentrated 50◦B juice, a constant increase in conductivity to 25◦B is reported despite that the overall ionicspecies are reduced. The significant decrease in viscosity between 50◦Band 25◦B, as previously reported, would be the main reason for thisconductivity increase. Enhanced mobility of charged species can beexpected as organic compounds (organic acids, anthocyanins, PACs) arepresent in lower concentrations when diluting the juice, which limitstheir potential association among themselves by hydrogen bonds andthe size of those charged species [15]. Dissociation of organic acids mayhave an additional conductivity effect in this way but are increasinglydiluted. From 25◦B to 5◦B, dissociation of organic acids still contributes to conductivity, but it can beassumed that ionic species dilution becomes dominant which explainsthe constant conductivity decrease until 5◦B. These results suggest thatan optimum between electrophoretic mobility (considering juice viscosity) and ionic species concentration (considering juice dilution effectand pH) has been obtained around 25◦B. 3.2. Effect of cranberry juice concentration on electrodialyticdeacidification process For the further deacidification trials, in addition to this presumablyoptimal juice concentration (25◦B), one above (35◦B) and few below(15◦B and 5◦B) were selected to form a concentration range and gaingeneral insight into the effect of juice concentration on the electrodialytic process. Their main properties are summarized in Table 3. 3.2.1. Juice and recovery solution parameter evolution 3.2.1.1. pH and conductivity. During ED and for all juice concentrationconditions, juice pH increased significantly (p < 0.001 for 5◦B and 15◦B,p = 0.009 for 25◦B, p = 0.025 for 35◦B) (Fig. 5). At the same time, itdecreased significantly for the recovery solution (p < 0.001 for 5◦B,p = 0.004 for 15◦B and 25◦B, p = 0.001 for 35◦B) (Fig. 5). Juice concentration had no significant impact (p = 0.634) on juice pH evolutionas a similar increase of 0.28–0.33 was reported.

As expected, 35◦B juicehad the lowest pH value, evolving from 2.13 ± 0.04 to 2.45 ± 0.15,while 5◦B juice had the highest pH value, starting from 2.39 ± 0.02 andending at 2.69 ± 0.05. It was interesting to mention that juice pH evolution seemed to deviate from a regular linear trend as the juice concentration used increased. For the recovery solution pH evolution, juiceconcentration conditions had also no significant impact (p = 0.847). ThepH decreased from an initial value of 4.75–5.23 (not shown in Fig. 5 forbetter graphical representation) to rapidly reach a plateau around 2.25in the very first minutes of the process. Those results are consistent withprevious studies performed on 8◦B cranberry juice. The regular production of OH-in the juice compartment by the BPM, and H+ generationin the recovery solution compartment, can explain the constant juice pHincrease and the rapid recovery solution pH decrease [10]. The organicacids are transferred through the AEM in their anionic dissociated formsaccording to the acid dissociation mechanism described by Martí-Calatayud et al. [34] and Rybalkina et al. [35].

Since the recovery solutionpH reached very quickly values under all pKa1 of the major organic acidsin cranberry juice (3.13 for citric acid, 3.46 for malic and quinic acid),these components were mainly recovered in a neutral form due to theircombination with H+ ions produced by the BPM, that no longer influence pH evolution.Juice conductivity evolved differently according to the juice concentration conditions (Fig. 5). At the beginning and as previously described,5◦B juice had the lowest conductivity values and 25◦B the highest. Inagreement with previous studies, 5◦B juice conductivity decreasedsignificantly (p < 0.001) from 2.29 ± 0.06 mS/cm to 1.76 ± 0.05 mS/cmbecause of negatively charged species migration (organic acids andminerals anions) through the AEM [10,36]. Leakage of some potassiumions through the BPM should also be considered, as reported by Serre et al.

content: [8]. For 15◦B and 25◦B juice, the conductivity remained quite steady (p =0.116 for 15◦B and p = 0.390 for 25◦B), while a slight significant increase(p = 0.012) was observed for 35◦B juice. Higher juice concentrationsinvolve a higher concentration of charged species, including positivelycharged compounds (anthocyanins, proanthocyanidins, and some minerals) that would not migrate through either the AEM or the BPM [10].These compounds in the 15◦B, 25◦B, and 35◦B juice could be in sufficientconcentration so that the conductivity decrease due to anions migrationsfrom the juice was not observed. Despite that potassium leakage couldprobably occurred from the juice as in previous studies, it seemed that thisphenomenon was not significant to impact juice conductivity. For therecovery solution, conductivity increased significantly for all concentration conditions (p < 0.001) and then reached a plateau around 7.5–8.5mS/cm. The rapid production of H+ ions by the BPM in the recovery solution induced the rapid conductivity increase at the beginning of theprocess. Interestingly, for the 25◦B juice condition, the conductivity tendsto increase more rapidly than the other one and to higher values. Themigration of organic acids and other charged compounds from the juice tothe recovery compartment could also increase the recovery solutionconductivity. A more efficient migration of these compounds from the25◦B juice due to higher initial conductivity could explain the trendobserved.

Considering pH results and organic acids pKa1 values, the H+ ions produced by the BPM are combined with organic acids in a neutralform that cannot impact anymore the solution conductivity, resulting inits plateauing. 3.2.1.2. Anthocyanin content. The individual and total concentration ofmajor anthocyanins found in cranberry juice was measured before andafter each ED treatment and is reported in Table 4. Regardless of thejuiceconcentration,peonidin-3-galactoside,cyanidin-3-galactoside,cyanidin-3-arabinoside and peonidin-3-arabinoside were the mostimportant anthocyanins found in the juice representing 95 % of the totalanthocyanincontent,whilepeonidin-3-glucosideandcyanidin-3glucoside were present in lower amounts. These proportions were verysimilar to the literature [8,36,37]. As previously observed for the totalpolyphenols content, the concentration of anthocyanins evolved linearlyat the beginning of the process according to juice concentration. However, while no significant differences were detected in anthocyanincontent for 5◦B and 15◦B juice before versus after treatment, this was notthesameforotherconcentrations.Asignificantdecrease(p = 0.005–0.029), representing a 6 %–8 % loss was detected for 25◦Bjuice, and a similar trend was reported for 35◦B juice (loss of 12 %–13%).

However, it applied only to a lowamount (<13 %) due to the positive charges of these compounds insolution that hinder their way to the AEM. It seemed that the greaterreduction observed with higher juice concentrations could result fromthe longer treatment duration. Anthocyanins are then more likely tointeract with membranes, especially when they are present in largerquantities. Transport by diffusion, due to the concentration differencebetween juice and recovery compartments, could be envisaged inaddition to their electromigration but in a very limited way regardingthe permselectivity of the ion exchange membranes and the presence ofelectromigration [44]. 3.2.1.3. Proanthocyanidin (PAC) content. Table 5 presents the concentration of PACs, from monomers to polymers, detected in both thecranberry juice and recovery solution before and after each ED treatment. The main compounds measured for each juice were the 2–3 mers,while no polymers were detected, unlike previous studies. Specific juicebatch composition affected by many factors (climatic conditions,cultivar, etc.) may explain this difference [45]. The concentration ofPACs evolved linearly according to juice concentration before ED whilea loss was observed for the most concentrated juices after ED, similarlyto the results obtained for anthocyanins but to a lower extent.

Areduction of PACs compounds in the juice of 3 %–5 % and 6 %–9 % wasrespectively observed for 25◦B and 35◦B juice but was not significant.On the other hand, a significant concentration was detected in the recovery solution for the 25◦B and 35◦B concentration conditions. Thiswas consistent from monomers to 4–6 mers but 7–10 mers seemed tobehave differently as no such compounds were detected in the recoverysolution. A more complex structure and lengthy molecules like 7–10mers would find more difficulty to be transported to the recovery solution compartment through the membranes.During cranberry juice deacidification by EDBM, previous researchdemonstrated that PACs content remained unchanged [8]. Precedentsuggested mechanisms for anthocyanins migration from the juicecompartment to the recovery one through the AEM may also be appliedto explain the PACs content evolution. As described before, the introduction of PACs in the AEM may occur in a delayed and slower mannerthan anthocyanins, which results in a lower loss in PACs compared toanthocyanins molecules.

Even though the juice qualities (organolepticand health-related properties) could be affected by a reduction in anthocyanins and PACs content, it should be important to mention that theloss obtained is quite small compared to the natural variability foundbetween juice batches [10,37]. 3.2.1.4. Organic acid content. Citric, quinic, and malic acids were themajor organic acids found in cranberry juice, while ascorbic and succinicacids were detected in minor proportions (Table 6), which is in agreementwith the literature [46]. The concentration of organic acids evolved linearly according to juice concentration at the beginning of the treatment,consistently with the density evolution reported before. Juice concentration had no significant effect (p = 0.430) on citric acid evolution as anaveraged 32 ± 3 % reduction (p < 0.001) was obtained considering allfour conditions. Similarly, the malic acid content in cranberry juice wasreduced by an average value of 61 ± 4 % after ED (p < 0.001), eventhoughslightvariationsweredetectedbetweentheconditions.

Regardless of the organic acid reported, the contentreduction in the juice corresponded to an approximately equivalentconcentration gain in the recovery solution, considering measurementerror, which suggests their migration through the AEM according to theED cell configuration (Fig. 1).Citric (molecular weight MW = 191.12 g/mol, pKa1 = 3.13,pKa2 = 4.76, pKa3 = 6.39) and malic acid (MW = 134.09 g/mol,pKa1 = 3.46, pKa2 = 5.05) are both known to easily migrate from thecranberry juice during ED process thanks to their high concentration andlow pKa values [46]. It is also known that several parameters may influence the ionic species transport rate through ion exchange membranes: the ionic compound concentration and its mobility in themembrane, related to its valence and size [44].

The migration of citricacid is relatively faster (twice as fast) than the migration of malic acid, asalready demonstrated during EDBM on both laboratory [8] and pilotscale [10]. The higher concentration of citric acid compared to otherorganic acids in cranberry juice, its low molecular weight, and pKawhich is the closest to the juice pH are the characteristics that have beensuggested to explain this difference. Despite that the results obtained inthe present work do not confirm those previous expectations, the migrations are relatively consistent with precedent study performed on thesame ED cell (EUR-2) [21]. The ratio of citric on malic acid content lossin the juice for a 40 % deacidification rate was calculated and gavesimilar values (1.3 according to Pellerin et al. [21] and 0.9–1.2 in thepresent work).Serre et al. [8] demonstrated that quinic acid (MW = 192.17 g/mol,pKa = 3.46) migrated only after 2 or 3 h of treatment at laboratory scalebecause of lower negative charges compared to citric or malic acid andthe presence of an aromatic carboxylic ring that hinder its migrationthrough the AEM.

A maximal loss of 13 ± 6 % of this compound was observed during the experiments for the 35◦B juicecondition.Similar phenomena associated with the initial juice concentrationandtreatmentdurationmayexplainthepresenceofsuccinic(MW = 118.09 g/mol, pKa1 = 4.21, pKa2 = 5.64) and ascorbic acid(MW = 176.12 g/mol, pKa1 = 4.04, pKa2 = 11.70) [48] in the recoverysolution after ED despite their high pKa values, which should preventthem from migrating, as already shown in precedent research [8,46]. Aspreviously discussed, the elevated pH of the AEM internal solution coulddeprotonate organic acids with relatively high pKa like ascorbic andsuccinic acid, which causes a negative charge of these acids and mayexplain their potential migration [40]. The total absence of succinic acidin the juice after treatment can be related to its very low initial amountcompared to other organic acids, that have entirely migrated.

As expected with visual observations, the lightness parameters (L*) decreased leading to darker color perception, the a*parameter moved toward red shades, and the b* parameter to yellowshades. The color may be associated with anthocyanins that havemigrated from the cranberry juice as it corroborates the usual shade ofthese polyphenolic compounds at the recovery solution pH (pH of2.2–2.3) [49]. In addition to the pH, anthocyanins color stability andintensity may vary according to their concentration and the presence ofother compounds (phenolic acids, organic acids, etc.) or themselves thatcan participate in copigmentation phenomenon [50]. These membranes were separating the electrode rinsing solution from the recovery one (Fig. 1).A constant decrease in conductivity of the electrode rinsing solution(varying from 16.1 ± 0.4 mS/cm to 9.7 ± 1.1 mS/cm) was reportedduring the process, especially when the treatment duration was thelongest. This observation would suggest the migration of Na+ andleakage of OH-ions that may result in higher membrane conductivity.The reuse of the same set of membranes can also explain this significantevolution. A decrease of 17 % in conductivity was observed for AEMafter treatment. Similar results were reported for cranberry juicedeacidification when using an MP-type cell (100 cm2 of effective surface) [38]. Organic acids and negatively charged compounds are passingthrough AEM during the ED process and may be involved in foulingdevelopment, as reported in the literature [40]. The colors of themembrane and the results previously presented about anthocyanins andPACs compounds (Tables 4 and 5) indicate that interactions of polyphenolic compounds with AEM may also be involved in the electricalconductivity decrease. Such a similar decrease should be expected forthe BPM, because of interactions with polyphenolic compounds andregarding the change in color of the membrane.

Global system resistance. A significant increase in resistance(p = 0.009 for 5◦B, p = 0.035 for 15◦B, p < 0.001 for 25◦B and 35◦B) wasreported during ED treatment for all conditions but to a greater extentwith higher juice concentrations (Fig. 9). For all juices (p = 0.256), aninitial resistance value of 10.8 ± 0.7 Ω was obtained, which reached values of 13.0 ± 0.2 Ω and 40.0 ± 0.0 Ω at the end of the process for the5◦B and 35◦B juice respectively. According to Ohm’s law (R = U/I),observed resistance increase in the experiment is firstly due to the increase in voltage with constant current intensity applied (galvanostaticmode), and afterward due to the decrease of current intensity appliedwith a constant voltage (24 V, potentiostatic mode).

content: ensity applied (galvanostaticmode), and afterward due to the decrease of current intensity appliedwith a constant voltage (24 V, potentiostatic mode). Since the conductivity of the juice and recovery solution remained steady throughout theED treatment, resistance increase is most probably caused by polyphenols interactions with membranes, as described in the previoussections for anthocyanins and PACs (section 3.2.1.2. and 3.2.1.3.). Also,it is known that organic acids can participate in fouling mechanisms byelectrostatic interactions with the AEM [40]. However, the reduction inconductivity of the electrode rinsing solution during the process (reported in section 3.2.2.1.) may contribute to the resistance growth. Forthe 5◦B juice, the voltage increase is low (<24 V) which explains theslight increase in resistance compared to higher juice concentrations.Consistent evolution was obtained when using a semi-industrial ED cellduring 95 h on a 8◦B juice and was explained by interactions betweencranberry polyphenols and the AEM [10]. This previous result indicatesthat treatment duration had a slower effect on fouling development inlow juice concentration conditions (5◦B - 8◦B), compared to higherconcentrations. A global linear relationship (r2 = 0.98) has been highlighted when considering all four conditions, suggesting that interactions and fouling mechanisms of membranes with polyphenols areoccurring similarly and continuously with the deacidification process.

Itis expected that parameters such as treatment duration and initial concentration of organic compounds (polyphenols, organic acids) in thejuice will be highly associated with fouling mechanisms and muchhigher resistance evolution, as observed for the 15◦B, 25◦B and 35◦Bjuices. The impact of higher concentrations of cranberry juice on systemresistance has never been reported in the literature, but a similar evolution was described in other fruit juices. A regular voltage increase wasobserved for deacidifying a concentrated passion fruit juice while aconstant evolution was reported for the clarified one, because a higherconcentration of organic compounds in the concentrated juice wasassociated with fouling, most probably occurring in the AEM, whichresulted in higher voltage applied [16].
